content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_006_page_001.jpg

1. Working together with him, then, we appeal to you not to receive the grace of God in vain.

2. For he says, "In a favorable time I listened to you, and in a day of salvation I have helped you." Behold, now is the favorable time; behold, now is the day of salvation.

3. We put no obstacle in anyone's way, so that no fault may be found with our ministry,

4. but as servants of God we commend ourselves in every way: by great endurance, in afflictions, hardships, calamities,

5. beatings, imprisonments, riots, labors, sleepless nights, hunger;

6. by purity, knowledge, patience, kindness, the Holy Spirit, genuine love;

7. by truthful speech, and the power of God; with the weapons of righteousness for the right hand and for the left;

8. through honor and dishonor, through slander and praise. We are treated as impostors, and yet are true;

9. as unknown, and yet well known; as dying, and behold, we live; as punished, and yet not killed;

10. as sorrowful, yet always rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, yet possessing everything.

11. We have spoken freely to you, Corinthians; our heart is wide open.

12. You are not restricted by us, but you are restricted in your own affections.

13. In return — I speak as to children — widen your hearts also.

14. Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers. For what partnership has righteousness with lawlessness? Or what fellowship has light with darkness?

15. What accord has Christ with Belial? Or what portion does a believer share with an unbeliever?

16. What agreement has the temple of God with idols? For we are the temple of the living God; as God said, "I will make my dwelling among them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people."

17. Therefore "go out from their midst, and be separate from them, says the Lord, and touch no unclean thing; then I will welcome you,

18. and I will be a father to you, and you shall be sons and daughters to me, says the Lord Almighty."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 6 contains Paul's most elaborate hardship catalogue (vv.3-10), a tender personal appeal (vv.11-13), and a call to holiness grounded in OT quotations (vv.14-18).

**Verses 8-10 — The antitheses:** All three translators preserved the paradoxical structure. SOPHIA affirms: these seven pairs are the rhetoric of the cross applied to apostolic life.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "working together with him" vs. "as God's partners"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "working together [with him] we also urge" | Literal συνεργοῦντες; "[with him]" supplied |
| LOGOS | "Working together with him, then, we appeal" | Same; formal phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "As God's partners, we beg you not to accept this marvelous gift of God's kindness and then ignore it" | "Partners" for συνεργοῦντες; "marvelous gift of God's kindness" for χάρις; "ignore" for εἰς κενόν |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's expansion adds "marvelous" and "kindness" (not in text) and changes "in vain" to "ignore" (different semantic field).

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds interpretive language.

---

### v.2 — "acceptable time" vs. "favorable time" vs. "just the right time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In an acceptable time I listened to you" | δεκτός as "acceptable" |
| LOGOS | "In a favorable time I listened to you" | δεκτός as "favorable" |
| PNEUMA | "At just the right time, I heard you" | Dynamic rendering; "just the right time" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. δεκτός means "acceptable/welcome/favorable." "Favorable" is the best English fit. PNEUMA's "just the right time" loses the theological sense of divine acceptance.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor word choice.

---

### vv.4-5 — Structure: verse-by-verse vs. combined

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | vv.4-5 as separate verses with semicolons | Preserves Greek list structure |
| LOGOS | vv.4-5 as separate verses; "riots" for ἀκαταστασίαι | Separate verses |
| PNEUMA | Combines vv.4-5 into one paragraph; "faced angry mobs" for ἀκαταστασίαι | Merged; narrative expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS structure (separate verses). PNEUMA's merging and expansion ("faced angry mobs") interprets ἀκαταστασίαι — which means "disturbances/tumults/instabilities" — as specifically hostile crowds.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA merges verses and narrows a general term.

---

### v.6 — "love unfeigned" vs. "genuine love" vs. "sincere love"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "love unfeigned" | ἀνυποκρίτῳ as "unfeigned" — archaic but precise |
| LOGOS | "genuine love" | Modern equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "sincere love" | Similar to LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS ("genuine love"). All three capture ἀνυπόκριτος ("without hypocrisy"). "Genuine" is the most natural modern English.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor word choice — archaic vs. modern.

---

### v.11 — "the mouth of us has opened" vs. "we have spoken freely" vs. "we have spoken honestly"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The mouth of us has opened to you, Korinthians; the heart of us has been enlarged" | Literal; preserves body imagery |
| LOGOS | "We have spoken freely to you, Corinthians; our heart is wide open" | Interprets "mouth opened" as "spoken freely" |
| PNEUMA | "We have spoken honestly with you, and our hearts are open to you" | "Honestly" for "freely"; adds "to you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Spoken freely" captures the sense of τὸ στόμα ἡμῶν ἀνέῳγεν (our mouth has opened) better than "honestly" (PNEUMA), which narrows the meaning to truthfulness rather than openness.

**Status:** Resolved. "Freely" vs. "honestly" — different aspects of the same metaphor.

---

### v.12 — "restricted in the affections of you" vs. "restricted in your own affections" vs. "withheld your love"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you are restricted in the affections of you" | σπλάγχνοις as "affections" (literally "bowels/intestines") |
| LOGOS | "you are restricted in your own affections" | Same; smoother |
| PNEUMA | "you have withheld your love from us" | Interprets σπλάγχνα as "love"; restructured |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. σπλάγχνα means "bowels/intestines" — used metaphorically for the seat of deep emotion (roughly "affections"). PNEUMA's "withheld your love" is exegetically sound but replaces the distinctive Pauline vocabulary.

**Status:** Resolved. σπλάγχνα is a characteristic Pauline term worth preserving.

---

### v.14 — "unequally yoked" vs. "don't team up"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not become unequally yoked with unbelievers" | ἑτεροζυγοῦντες preserved |
| LOGOS | "Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers" | Same metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "Don't team up with those who are unbelievers" | "Team up" replaces agricultural metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The yoking metaphor (from Deut 22:10) is important — it evokes agricultural law about not pairing different kinds of animals. PNEUMA's "team up" loses this intertextual connection.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA drops a significant OT allusion.

---

### v.15 — "Beliar" vs. "Belial" vs. "the devil"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Beliar" | Follows Greek Βελιάρ |
| LOGOS | "Belial" | Hebrew form of the name |
| PNEUMA | "the devil" | Interpretive; replaces proper name |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS ("Belial"). The proper name from Jewish apocalyptic tradition should be preserved. PNEUMA's "the devil" replaces a specific name with a generic title, losing the intertextual connection to Second Temple Judaism.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA replaces a proper name with a generic title.

---

### v.17 — "touch no unclean thing" vs. "don't touch their filthy things"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not touch [the] unclean [thing]" | Literal ἀκαθάρτου |
| LOGOS | "touch no unclean thing" | Formal; preserves OT language |
| PNEUMA | "Don't touch their filthy things" | "Filthy" — colloquial; adds "their" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Unclean" is the standard biblical term (from Isa 52:11). PNEUMA's "filthy" changes the register and adds "their" (possessive not in Greek).

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA shifts the register from biblical to colloquial.

---

*Summary: 8 disagreements logged. No ⚠️ flagged — all are structural/stylistic. Most significant: v.14 (yoking metaphor vs. "team up"), v.15 ("Belial" vs. "the devil"), v.12 (σπλάγχνα — Pauline vocabulary).*

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
